news.title: How FIFA turned the football World Cup into a $15 billion money machine
news.lede: Spain may have lifted the FIFA World Cup trophy, but the tournament's biggest winner never stepped onto the pitch.The Spanish players celebrated with medals around their necks and millions of dollars in prize money after beating Argentina in the final. Across the tournament, FIFA distributed nearly $1 billion in prize money to the 48 participating teams, rewarding performances on the field.Away from the stadiums, however, another number stood out. More than $15 billion.That's how much FIFA now expects to generate from its 2023-2026 commercial cycle, making the 2026 World Cup the most lucrative tournament in its history. Just a year ago, the governing body had projected revenues of around $8.9 billion before repeatedly revising its estimates upward as commercial demand exceeded expectations.The tournament showed that the modern World Cup is no longer just football's biggest spectacle.

news.title: Spain top FIFA rankings, Saudi 58th after World Cup
news.lede: DUBAI: The Saudi Arabian national football team have jumped three places in the FIFA World Rankings, which were announced this week following the World Cup. Spain, who beat Argentina 1-0 on Sunday to claim their second World Cup triumph, climbed to top spot. The Saudi team are now in 58th place globally, and fifth in Asia. The highest-ranked nations from the Asian Football Federation are Japan in 17th, Iran (22nd), Australia (28th), and South Korea (32nd).

news.title: The president of the Argentine Football Association breaks his silence: do not be fooled by "conspiracy theories"
news.lede: Tapia admits that Spain deserved the 2026 World Cup title. The 2026 World Cup final won't be forgotten in a hurry. Not just for the joy that swept across Spain after the coronation, but for the wave of controversy and the hundreds of conspiracy theories that erupted in Argentina after Ferran Torres's goal stripped the "tango dancers" of their world crown. Explanations for the defeat have spread like wildfire in Argentina over the past hours.

news.title: World Cup 2026: Fabian Ruiz and Gavi get weight in tomatoes for win
news.lede: Ruiz (left) and Gavi were greeted by a large crowd on their return to Los Palacios y Villafranca. Winning the World Cup brings many rewards for players... but receiving your own body weight in tomatoes is surely one of the more unusual. That is what Spain midfielders Fabian Ruiz and Gavi were presented with when they returned to their hometown after being part of the Spain squad that triumphed in North America. The pair come from Los Palacios y Villafranca - a town to the south of Seville that has a population lower than 40,000 - and returned there on Wednesday for an official reception at the town hall.

news.title: The lie of the beautiful game and a mystery that killed the Arab dream.. 10 lessons learned from the 2026 World Cup
news.lede: The World Cup that revealed the truth: the tournament that changed the rules of the game. The 2026 World Cup has closed on one of the richest editions for events and stories in the tournament's history. The first finals to feature 48 teams brought striking shifts that redrew parts of the world game, and laid bare truths hidden behind the results and figures. Arab and African teams struggled in the decisive moments.

news.title: ‘A surprising success’: US, Mexico and Canada fans on hosting the World Cup
news.lede: The joy, love, hope and fun provided by fans outweighed the commercialism, extortionate prices and ugly politics. This summer has been so vibrant and full of life. I had my doubts but, as the World Cup approached and Boston got ready to host its first game, I started feeling the excitement and enthusiasm in the air. That mainly had to do with the huge numbers of Scottish fans who arrived for their match against Haiti.

news.title: Gavi calls for leniency for Paredes after ugly World Cup final bust-up
